The consumer has to decide how to... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 2.1 UTILITY, 2.1.1 Cardinal Utility Analysis, MU5 = TU5 – TU4 = 30 – 28 = 2, TUn = MU1 + MU2 + … + MUn-1 + MUn. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Consumer Behaviour is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that chapter 2 Theor Theor Theor Theor Theory of y of y of y of y of Consumer Behaviour Consumer Behaviour Consumer Behaviour Consumer Behaviour Consumer Behaviour In this chapter, we will study the behaviour of an individual consumer.

The text explains that the consumer has to decide how to spend her income on different goods1.

The text explains that most naturally, any consumer will want to get a combination of goods that gives her maximum satisfaction.

The text explains that this depends on the likes of the consumer and what the consumer can afford to buy.

The text explains that the ‘likes’ of the consumer are also called ‘preferences’.

The text explains that and what the consumer can afford to buy, depends on prices of the goods and the income of the consumer.

The text explains that this chapter presents two different approaches that explain consumer behaviour (i) Cardinal Utility Analysis and (ii) Ordinal Utility Analysis.

The text explains that preliminary Notations and Assumptions A consumer, in general, consumes many goods; but for simplicity, we shall consider the consumer’s choice problem in a situation where there are only two goods2: bananas and mangoes.

The text explains that any combination of the amount of the two goods will be called a consumption bundle or, in short, a bundle.

The text explains that in general, we shall use the variable x1 to denote the quantity of bananas and x2 to denote the quantity of mangoes. x1 and x2 can be positive or zero.

The text explains that (x1, x2) would mean the bundle consisting of x1 quantity of bananas and x2 quantity of mangoes.

The text explains that for particular values of x1 and x2, (x1, x2), would give us a particular bundle.

The text explains that for example, the bundle (5,10) consists of 5 bananas and 10 mangoes; the bundle (10, 5) consists of 10 bananas and 5 mangoes.

The text explains that 2.1 UTILITY A consumer usually decides his demand for a commodity on the basis of utility (or satisfaction) that he derives from it.

The text explains that utility of a commodity is its want-satisfying capacity.

The text explains that the more the need of a commodity or the stronger the desire to have it, the greater is the utility derived from the commodity.

The text explains that an explaination for a downward sloping demand curve rests on the notion of diminishing marginal utility.

The text explains that hence, the law of diminishing marginal utility explains why demand curves have a negative slope.

The text explains that indifference curve slopes downwards from left to right: An indifference curve slopes downwards from left to right, which means that in order to have more of bananas, the consumer has to forego some mangoes.

The text explains that to explain this, let us allow two indifference curves to intersect each other as shown in the figure 2.8.

The text explains that the negative slope of the demand curve can also be explained in terms of the two effects namely, substitution effect and income effect that come into play when price of a commodity changes.

The text explains that thus, a consumer’s demand for a normal good moves in the same direction as the income of the consumer.

The text explains that however, there are some goods the demands for which move in the opposite direction of the income of the consumer.

The text explains that in such a case, the substitution effect and the income effect will work in opposite directions.

The text explains that in general, the demand for a good moves in the opposite direction of the price of its complementary goods.

The text explains that the demand for a good usually moves in the direction of the price of its substitutes.

The text explains that for normal goods, the demand curve shifts rightward and for inferior goods, the demand curve shifts leftward.

The text explains that if there is an increase in the price of a substitute good, the demand curve shifts rightward.

The text explains that if the consumer’s preferences change in favour of a good, the demand curve for such a good shifts rightward.

The text explains that the demand curve for ice-creams, for example, is likely to shift rightward in the summer because of preference for ice-creams goes up in summer.

The text explains that the demand curve in panel (a) shifts leftward and that in panel (b) shifts rightward.

The text explains that 2.6 ELASTICITY OF DEMAND The demand for a good moves in the opposite direction of its price.

The text explains that at the midpoint of the demand curve, the elasticity is 1, at any point to the left of the midpoint, it is greater than 1 and at any point to the right, it is less than 1.

The text explains that the expenditure on the good would change in the opposite direction as the price change if and only if the percentage change in quantity is greater than the percentage change in price, ie if the good is price-elastic (see rows 2 and 4 in table 2.5).

The text explains that the expenditure on the good would change in the same direction as the price change if and only if the percentage change in quantity is less than the percentage change in price, i.e., if the good is price inelastic (see rows 1 and 5 in table 2.5).

The text explains that explain why the budget line is downward sloping.

The text explains that questions 5, 6 and 7 are related to question 4.

The text explains that give examples of two goods which are substitutes of each other.

The text explains that give examples of two goods which are complements of each other.

The text explains that at price Rs 4, the demand for the good is 25 units.

The text explains that suppose price of the good increases to Rs 5, and as a result, the demand for the good falls to 20 units.

The text explains that suppose the price elasticity of demand for a good is – 0.2.

The text explains that if there is a 5 % increase in the price of the good, by what percentage will the demand for the good go down?

The text explains that how will the expenditure on the good be affected if there is a 10 % increase in the price of the good?

The text explains that suppose there was a 4 % decrease in the price of a good, and as a result, the expenditure on the good increased by 2 %.

The text explains that what can you say about the elasticity of demand?

Source: NCERT Class 12 Introductory Microeconomics, Chapter 2: Consumer Behaviour

concise_notes.definition: This chapter presents two different approaches that explain consumer behaviour (i) Cardinal Utility Analysis and (ii) Ordinal Utility Analysis.
2.1.1 Cardinal Utility Analysis Cardinal utility analysis assumes that level of utility can be expressed in numbers.

prelims_traps: Do not treat 2.1 UTILITY and 2.1.1 Cardinal Utility Analysis as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Consumer Behaviour; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Consumer Behaviour through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 2.1 UTILITY and 2.1.1 Cardinal Utility Analysis to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
